name: List Data Structures
description: Test your knowledge on the core concepts of lists, stacks, and queues, essential building blocks in computer science.

questions.question: Which data structure allows elements to be stored contiguously or non-contiguously and accessed by index or reference?
questions.explain: A list is a flexible data structure that can store elements either contiguously (like an array-based list) or non-contiguously (like a linked list).

questions.question: What principle governs the order in which elements are added to and removed from a stack?
questions.explain: A stack operates on the Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) principle, meaning the last element added is the first one to be removed.

questions.question: Which principle describes the behavior of a queue, where the first element added is the first one to be removed?
questions.explain: A queue follows the First-In-First-Out (FIFO) principle, similar to people waiting in a line.

questions.question: What is the operation called when an element is added to the top of a stack?
questions.explain: The 'push' operation adds an element to the top of a stack.

questions.question: Which operation removes an element from the front of a queue?
questions.explain: The 'dequeue' operation removes an element from the front of a queue.

questions.question: Which feature distinguishes a dynamic list from a static array?
questions.explain: Dynamic lists can adjust their capacity during program execution, unlike static arrays which have a fixed size.

questions.question: In which programming context is a stack commonly used?
questions.explain: Function calls and their local variables are typically managed using a call stack in most programming languages.

questions.question: A queue is best suited for which of the following real-world applications?
questions.explain: Queues are ideal for scenarios where tasks need to be processed in the order they were received, like print queues or task scheduling.

questions.question: What does the acronym LIFO stand for in the context of data structures?
questions.explain: LIFO is an abbreviation for Last-In-First-Out, which describes the behavior of a stack.

questions.question: In data structures, what does FIFO represent?
questions.explain: FIFO stands for First-In-First-Out, characteristic of a queue where elements are processed in insertion order.
